lessons.title: Parts of Speech
lessons.description: Learn Parts of Speech with focused discussion, examples, activity, and a 5-item quiz.
objectives: Understand key ideas in Parts of Speech
Analyze topic-specific examples
Apply learning through the activity
Answer quiz items with evidence-based reasoning

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: This lesson explores Parts of Speech for Grade 7 English learners.

sections.heading: Main Discussion
sections.content:
Parts of speech are the basic categories of words in English. Nouns name people, places, things, or ideas; verbs show action or being; adjectives describe nouns; and adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. Each part of speech has a job in a sentence.

For example, in the sentence 'The careful student writes clearly,' student is a noun, writes is a verb, careful is an adjective, and clearly is an adverb. Knowing each word's job helps you understand how the sentence is built and what it means.

Parts of speech matter because clear sentences depend on words being used correctly. When you know how nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs work, you can read more accurately and write sentences that are easier to understand.

audioNarration.transcript: Narration for Parts of Speech: Parts of speech are the basic categories of words in English. Nouns name people, places, things, or ideas; verbs show action or being; adjectives describe nouns; and adverbs describe verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. Each part of speech has a job in a sentence.

sections.heading: Visual/Animated Explanation
sections.content: Visual guide for Parts of Speech.

sections.heading: Guided Examples
items: Identify the main idea in a text about Parts of Speech.
Pick the best supporting details.
Write an inference with textual evidence.

terms.meaning: Central thought of a text.
Information that explains the main idea.
Conclusion based on clues.
Writer attitude toward topic.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: This lesson on Parts of Speech covered the key ideas, examples, activity, and quiz feedback.

activity.instruction: Match each part of speech with its job in a sentence.

items.answer: Names a person, place, thing, or idea.
Describes a verb, adjective, or another adverb.

quiz.question: What does a noun do?
choices: Names a person, place, thing, or idea
Shows only action
Describes a verb only
Joins two paragraphs
quiz.answer: Names a person, place, thing, or idea
quiz.explanation: A noun names people, places, things, or ideas.

quiz.question: In "The careful student writes clearly," which word is the verb?
choices: writes
careful
student
clearly
quiz.answer: writes
quiz.explanation: Writes shows the action.

quiz.question: In the same sentence, which word is an adjective?
choices: careful
writes
clearly
The
quiz.answer: careful
quiz.explanation: Careful describes the noun student.

quiz.question: Which word is an adverb in "The careful student writes clearly"?
choices: clearly
student
careful
The
quiz.answer: clearly
quiz.explanation: Clearly describes how the student writes.

quiz.question: Why do parts of speech matter?
choices: They help readers understand sentence structure and meaning
They make all words the same
They remove verbs from sentences
They avoid clear writing
quiz.answer: They help readers understand sentence structure and meaning
quiz.explanation: Knowing word jobs improves reading and writing.

lessons.depedNote: Sample content inspired by selected DepEd competencies for Grade 7 English.

lessons.title: Reading for Main Ideas
lessons.description: Learn Reading for Main Ideas with focused discussion, examples, activity, and a 5-item quiz.
objectives: Understand key ideas in Reading for Main Ideas
Analyze topic-specific examples
Apply learning through the activity
Answer quiz items with evidence-based reasoning

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: This lesson explores Reading for Main Ideas for Grade 7 English learners.

sections.heading: Main Discussion
sections.content:
Reading for main ideas means finding the most important point an author wants to communicate. The main idea is different from a detail. A detail supports, explains, or gives an example of the main idea, but it is not the whole message of the text.

To find the main idea, ask: What is this paragraph or text mostly about? Look at the title, repeated words, topic sentences, and important details. For example, if a paragraph gives several details about saving water, the main idea may be that people should conserve water every day.

Reading for main ideas matters because it helps you understand texts faster and more accurately. It also helps you summarize, answer questions, and separate important information from extra details.

audioNarration.transcript: Narration for Reading for Main Ideas: Reading for main ideas means finding the most important point an author wants to communicate. The main idea is different from a detail. A detail supports, explains, or gives an example of the main idea, but it is not the whole message of the text.

sections.content: Visual guide for Reading for Main Ideas.

Write an inference with textual evidence.

terms.meaning: Central thought of a text.
Information that explains the main idea.
Conclusion based on clues.
Writer attitude toward topic.

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: This lesson on Reading for Main Ideas covered the key ideas, examples, activity, and quiz feedback.

activity.instruction: Arrange the learning flow for Reading for Main Ideas.
items: Identify how finding is defined in Reading for Main Ideas.
Describe the key point step taught in the discussion.
Apply author in a concrete situation from the lesson.
Explain the wants based reason why Reading for Main Ideas matters.
correctOrder: Identify how finding is defined in Reading for Main Ideas.
Describe the key point step taught in the discussion.
Apply author in a concrete situation from the lesson.
Explain the wants based reason why Reading for Main Ideas matters.

quiz.question: What is the main idea?
choices: The most important point of a text
One small example only
The last word of a text
A random detail
quiz.answer: The most important point of a text
quiz.explanation: The main idea is what the text is mostly about.

quiz.question: How is a detail different from the main idea?
choices: A detail supports or explains the main idea
A detail is always the title
A detail replaces the whole text
A detail has no connection
quiz.answer: A detail supports or explains the main idea
quiz.explanation: Details give support, examples, or explanation.

quiz.question: Which clue can help identify the main idea?
choices: Repeated words
Page color only
Font size only
The number of commas only
quiz.answer: Repeated words
quiz.explanation: Repeated words often show what the text emphasizes.

quiz.question: If a paragraph gives many details about saving water, what may be its main idea?
choices: People should conserve water every day
Water has no use
Only milk tea matters
Sentences need verbs
quiz.answer: People should conserve water every day
quiz.explanation: The details point to water conservation.

quiz.question: True or False: finding is a key lesson concept in Reading for Main Ideas.
quiz.answer: True
quiz.explanation: The discussion identifies finding as part of the lesson understanding.

sections.heading: Introduction

sections.heading: Main Discussion
sections.content:
Context Clues is an important topic because it builds a clear definition of the lesson ideas and connects them to academic tasks. In this discussion, students learn how Context Clues works and why it appears in real classroom and community situations.

A strong understanding of Context Clues requires explicit strategy and careful use of terms. For example, a focused example shows how the concept is applied, how reasoning is checked, and how mistakes can be corrected with evidence.
